question:
Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?52-year-old middle-aged male.
Medical History:?Over 5 years ago, the patient developed left knee swelling and pain without an obvious inciting cause, predominantly on the lateral side. Pain aggravated after walking or exercise and was alleviated by rest. No fever or chills. The patient sought treatment at a local hospital and was given oral medication, but reported suboptimal results.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?Mild swelling of the left knee; a mass approximately 1.5cm x 1.5cm was visible in the anterolateral space. No erythema or ecchymosis on the skin. Palpation:?Skin temperature of the left knee was not elevated. The anterolateral mass was soft, with clear boundaries; tenderness was present in the surrounding joint space. Dorsalis pedis artery pulsation was palpable. Sensation in bilateral lower limbs was symmetrical without hypoesthesia. Movement:?Left knee ROM: 0ˇă-140ˇă. Floating patella test (-), Lateral McMurray sign (-), Anterior Drawer Test (-), Posterior Drawer Test (-), Lachman test (-), Valgus stress test (-), Varus stress test (-); Hyperflexion test (-); Hyperextension test (-). Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Left Knee (Plain Scan):?Left knee alignment is acceptable; osteophytosis and sclerosis of articular surfaces and margins. Morphology and signal of ACL, PCL, MCL, and LCL show no significant abnormalities. Linear increased signal seen in the lateral meniscus reaching the articular surface, with an adjacent cystic high signal. No significant abnormal signal in the medial meniscus. Small amount of joint effusion.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?55-year-old middle-aged female.
Medical History:?Over 10 years ago, the patient developed right knee pain with restricted mobility without an obvious inciting cause. Pain aggravated after prolonged walking, particularly when using stairs, and was alleviated by rest. Conservative treatment at a local hospital yielded average results. Over 1 year ago, the aforementioned symptoms worsened.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?No swelling, pallor, shine, erythema, obvious deformity, or ulceration of the skin on bilateral knees. Palpation:?No palpable masses. Skin temperature normal. Positive tenderness in the right lateral knee joint space. Dorsalis pedis artery pulsation palpable. No obvious sensory deficit. Movement:?Right knee ROM 0ˇă-100ˇă. Right knee floating patella test negative, anterior/posterior drawer tests negative, varus/valgus stress tests negative. Right knee grinding test positive, lateral McMurray sign positive. Right knee hyperextension and hyperflexion tests positive. Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Right Knee (Plain Scan):?Right knee alignment is acceptable; partial marginal osteophytosis; patellofemoral cartilage signal is heterogeneous and locally thinned. Linear high signal in the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus, reaching the articular surface; medial meniscus signal is normal. No significant abnormal high signal in ACL, PCL, collateral ligaments, or patellar ligament. Small amount of joint effusion.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?39-year-old young female.
Medical History:?2 years ago, the patient developed left knee pain without an obvious inciting cause. No low-grade fever, night sweats, other joint discomfort, or morning stiffness. Relieved by rest; no specific treatment was sought. The pain later recurred, especially during weight-bearing activities and using stairs.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?No swelling or deformity of bilateral knees; no erythema or ecchymosis. Palpation:?Skin temperature of bilateral knees was not elevated. Tenderness in the left lateral joint space; no tenderness in the medial space. Dorsalis pedis artery pulsation was palpable. Sensation in bilateral lower limbs was symmetrical without hypoesthesia. Movement:?Left knee ROM: 10ˇă-140ˇă. Floating patella test (-), Lateral McMurray sign (+), Anterior Drawer Test (-), Posterior Drawer Test (-), Lachman test (-), Varus/Valgus stress tests (-). Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Left Knee (Plain Scan):?Right knee is in position; articular marginal osteophytosis; patch-like long T1 and long T2 signals in the marrow cavity of the distal femur and tibial plateau. Right lateral discoid meniscus is slightly displaced medially; strip-like long T2 signals in the anterior horn, body, and posterior horn, partially reaching the articular surface. Patch-like slightly long T2 signal in the anterior horn of the medial meniscus, reaching the articular surface; posterior horn of the medial meniscus is unremarkable. ACL and PCL show natural courses without abnormal signal. Medial and lateral collateral ligaments are unremarkable. Patch-like slightly long T2 signal in the patellar ligament. Edema of the infrapatellar fat pad; small-to-moderate effusion in the suprapatellar bursa; small amount of effusion in the knee joint cavity. Fluid in the popliteal gastrocnemius medial head-semimembranosus bursa.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?36-year-old young male.
Medical History:?2 years ago, the patient twisted his right knee playing basketball and was diagnosed with a 'right anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) (ACL) rupture'. He underwent 'right ACL reconstruction' at another hospital. 4 months ago, he twisted the right knee again while playing basketball, resulting in pain and slight swelling. Denies lower limb numbness or sensory disturbance. He sought immediate care at a local hospital and received only symptomatic pain management. After the pain resolved, the patient felt worsened instability in the right knee and was afraid to perform rapid running or stopping movements.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?No obvious swelling or deformity of the right knee; no erythema, ecchymosis, or pigmentation. Several healed surgical scars of varying sizes were visible on the right knee. Palpation:?Skin temperature not elevated. No tenderness in the right medial joint space; no obvious tenderness in the lateral space. Dorsalis pedis artery palpable. Sensation symmetrical, no hypoesthesia. Movement:?Right knee ROM: 0ˇă-130ˇă. Floating patella test (-), Medial McMurray sign (-), Patellofemoral grinding test (-), Right Anterior Drawer Test (+), Posterior Drawer Test (-), Right Lachman test (+), Varus/Valgus stress tests (-), Hyperextension/Hyperflexion tests (-). No other obvious abnormalities. Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Right Knee (Plain Scan):?Post-operative status of bilateral ACL reconstruction: Internal fixation devices, susceptibility artifacts, and bone tunnel shadows seen in the lateral epicondyles of the bilateral femurs and medial proximal tibias. The reconstructed ACL in the right knee is tortuous; the femoral attachment end is swollen with increased fat-suppressed T2 signal and partial discontinuity. The reconstructed ACL in the left knee is currently continuous with no significant abnormal signal. Anterior-posterior alignment of the right tibiofemoral joint is suboptimal; left knee is in position. Mild osteophytosis in constituent bones of both knees; synovial thickening in both knees. Small amount of fluid signal in the suprapatellar bursa and joint cavity of both knees, more pronounced on the right. Linear fat-suppressed T2 slightly high signal in the posterior horn of the right medial meniscus and patch-like signal in the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus, not reaching the articular surface or capsular margin. The left medial meniscus is thinner with patch-like fat-suppressed T2 slightly high signal in the body, reaching the articular surface. Strip-like long T2 signal in the right quadriceps tendon and right LCL. Morphology and signal of PCLs, left collateral ligaments, right MCL, and bilateral patellar ligaments are unremarkable. No significant abnormal signal in the soft tissues surrounding both knees.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?35-year-old young male.
Medical History:?Over 10 years ago, the patient fell and sprained the left knee, causing pain and swelling. Oral medication at a local hospital relieved the swelling and pain, but left knee instability persisted, especially during sports. 1 month ago, the patient felt worsened pain and severely restricted activity.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?No obvious swelling of the left knee; no erythema or ecchymosis. Palpation:?Skin temperature not elevated. No tenderness in medial or lateral joint spaces. Dorsalis pedis artery palpable. Sensation symmetrical, no hypoesthesia. Movement:?Left knee ROM: 0ˇă-140ˇă. Floating patella test (-), Medial McMurray sign (-), Anterior Drawer Test (+), Posterior Drawer Test (-), Lachman test (+), Varus/Valgus stress tests (-). Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Left Knee (Plain Scan):?Left knee is in position; no significant abnormal bone signal in constituent bones. Morphology and signal of medial and lateral menisci are unremarkable. Mid-segment of the left ACL is discontinuous with poor tension; increased T2 signal within and around it. Fiber bundles of the PCL and tibial/fibular collateral ligaments are clearly visualized without abnormal signal. No swelling of periarticular soft tissues; small amount of fluid signal in the suprapatellar bursa, deep infrapatellar bursa, and joint cavity.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?24-year-old young female.
Medical History:?12 years ago, the patient first twisted her right knee while dancing, feeling a lateral patellar dislocation sensation and pain. Fixation for 4 weeks and conservative treatment for 2 weeks alleviated symptoms. The injury recurred 3 times. 1 year ago, another twist occurred with a sensation of instability and patellar wobbling. The patient cannot perform sports or rapid turns/stops, but can walk and use stairs normally.
Physical Examination:?Right quadriceps atrophy. Increased right patellar mobility compared to normal. Increased Q-angle. Patellar apprehension test positive. 
MRI of the Right Knee (Plain Scan):?Right patellofemoral alignment is suboptimal; tibiofemoral alignment is acceptable; minimal bone marrow edema under the tibial intercondylar eminence. Cartilage on the medial margin of the patella is irregular and partially absent; subchondral bone is irregular. Femoral trochlear groove is shallow. Medial patellar retinaculum is thickened with increased signal. Morphology and signal of ACL, PCL, and collateral ligaments are unremarkable. No significant abnormal signal in medial or lateral menisci. Minimal joint effusion.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?36-year-old young male.
Medical History:?9 months ago, following fractures of the left tibial plateau, patella, and fibular head, and injuries to the ACL, PCL, and MCL, the patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the tibial plateau. Due to poor wound healing, debridement and VSD (Vacuum Sealing Drainage) were performed. After VSD removal and healing, brace fixation was used for 6 weeks. Subsequently, significant flexion/extension limitation was noted (extension straight, flexion to 20ˇă). Despite rehabilitation (passive flexion up to 80ˇă), stiffness worsened. Diagnosed with left knee subluxation and stiffness, he underwent open reduction and fixation. 6 weeks post-op, pin removal was performed, but stiffness worsened further.
Physical Examination:?Three old surgical scars visible on the left knee. Restricted mobility: Active flexion 20ˇă, Passive flexion 60ˇă, Extension 110ˇă. Patellar grind test positive. Medial joint space tenderness positive. 
MRI of the Left Knee (Plain Scan):?Post-operative status of comminuted left tibial plateau fracture; significant metal artifacts; depression of the medial articular surface. Patch-like high FS-PDWI signal in the distal left femur, with heterogeneous signal. ACL and PCL are identifiable. Edema of soft tissues surrounding the left knee, presenting as heterogeneous high FS-PDWI signal. Patella is Wiberg type 2; patellofemoral alignment is fair. No significant effusion in the suprapatellar bursa or joint cavity.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?75-year-old elderly female.
Medical History:?Over 10 years ago, the patient developed intermittent, needle-like bilateral knee pain after activity, aggravated by prolonged walking. Relieved by rest. No chills, fever, or night sweats. Self-treatment with hot towels was ineffective. Over 3 months ago, left knee pain worsened with restricted mobility, affecting walking (distance ~200m) and quality of life.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?Limping gait. Lower limbs equal length. No ulceration, sinus tracts, or purulence. Pale blue ecchymosis visible. No muscle atrophy or varicose veins. Slight swelling of knees. Palpation:?Skin temperature normal. Soft tissue tension normal. Patellar tenderness (medial/lateral) (+). Left dorsalis pedis pulse weak; right pulse normal. Bilateral posterior tibial pulses normal. Sensation normal. Movement:?Left knee Flexion 100ˇă, Extension 0ˇă, Varus 0ˇă; pain induced by motion (+). Right knee Flexion 110ˇă, Extension 0ˇă, Varus 0ˇă; pain induced by motion (+). Muscle strength/tone normal. Pathological reflexes not elicited. 
Digital X-ray of Bilateral Knees (AP/Lateral):?Bilateral knee osteoarthritis; low bone density in bilateral knee joints.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:
Demographics:?55-year-old middle-aged male.
Medical History:?1 month ago, the patient twisted the right knee, causing immediate pain and swelling. Topical treatment was applied. After recovery, he occasionally experienced 'giving way' while walking.
Physical Examination: Inspection:?No obvious swelling; no rash or effusion. Posterior sagging of the right tibial plateau observed. Palpation:?Skin temperature normal. No tenderness in joint spaces. No mass. Dorsalis pedis artery palpable. Sensation normal. Movement:?Right knee ROM: 0ˇă-130ˇă. Floating patella test (-), McMurray sign (-), Anterior Drawer Test (-), Posterior Drawer Test (+), Lachman test (-), Stress tests (-). Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
MRI of the Right Knee (Plain Scan):?Morphology of anterior/posterior horns of right medial and lateral menisci is fair, with internal strip-like slightly high PD signal not reaching the articular surface. High PD signal in the area of right ACL, LCL, and patellar retinacula; PCL structure is poorly visualized and locally discontinuous with increased T2WI signal; remaining ligaments unremarkable. Cartilage of right femur, tibia, and patella is thinned with reduced signal and rough margins. Patch-like high PD signal with blurred margins in subchondral bone of right tibial plateau, distal femur, and patella; osteophytosis of remaining bones; joint alignment acceptable. Synovial thickening in right knee; effusion in joint cavity, suprapatellar bursa, and surrounding bursae; multiple nodular bone-like signals with clear margins in periarticular soft tissues; strip-like high PD signal in right infrapatellar fat pad.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

Pain was distinct after prolonged sitting/walking and squatting, and worse when leaning forward while seated. Condition has not progressed but affects daily life.

Recovery was fair. 1 year ago, left hip pain recurred with limping and difficulty walking. Oral medication provided slight relief. Pain worsened; currently walking distance ~500m, difficulty with stairs/shoes, sitting tolerance <30 mins. Harris score: 42.

Tenderness at left femoral head projection (+). Greater trochanter percussion pain (+). Sensation/blood supply good. Movement:?Left hip restricted: Extension 0ˇă, Flexion 40ˇă, Abduction 10ˇă, Adduction 0ˇă, Int/Ext Rotation 0ˇă. Pain induced by abduction/rotation (+). FABER (+). Right hip ROM acceptable. FABER (-). Pathological reflexes negative. Measurement:?Bilateral lower limbs were essentially equal in length. 
Digital X-ray of the Left Femoral Neck (Oblique):?Pelvic tilt; left femoral head/neck internal fixation in position, no signs of fracture or loosening; left femoral head morphology abnormal, cortical bone rough, density heterogeneously increased with multiple patch-like slightly low density areas; left femoral neck shortened; left hip articular surface proliferation and sclerosis, joint space narrowed; findings suggest possible left femoral head avascular necrosis and left hip osteoarthritis; please correlate with clinical findings. Bilateral sacroiliac joints and right hip in position, degenerative changes.

Based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and imaging findings, please answer the following questions:

Medical History:?Over 10 years ago, the patient developed right shoulder pain without an inciting cause. Pain with activity. Accompanied by weakness and restricted mobility. NSAIDs provided relief. 5 years ago, a fall caused recurrence. Recent worsening affects quality of life.
Physical Examination:?No swelling/deformity. Greater tuberosity tenderness. Abduction 100ˇă, Internal Rotation 70ˇă. ABERR: Grade 3, ABIRR: Grade 5, Hug-up: Grade 2, Bear hug: Grade 3, Hug-off: Grade 3, Lift-off: (+), Whipple: Grade 4, Belly press test: (+), Jobe test: (+), Full can test: (+). 
MRI of the Right Shoulder (Plain Scan):?Right shoulder alignment is acceptable; osteophytosis and sclerosis of greater tuberosity, acromion, and acromioclavicular joint; multiple small cysts in humeral head. Type II acromion. Discontinuity of supraspinatus and subscapularis tendons with retraction, extending to the anterior margin of the infraspinatus tendon; partial signal increase in infraspinatus tendon. Teres minor tendon unremarkable. Dislocation of long head of biceps, tendon poorly visualized. Small amount of effusion in subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Small amount of joint cavity effusion.
Please output personalized treatment recommendations for this patient that adhere to the principles of evidence-based medicine.

MRI of the Right Shoulder (Plain Scan):?Right shoulder alignment is acceptable; osteophytosis of acromion, greater tuberosity, and glenoid. Posterosuperior humeral head bone is irregular and slightly depressed. Small cyst in anterior humeral head. Acromioclavicular joint signal slightly high. Supraspinatus tendon continuity interrupted (full thickness), stump retracted to medial top of humeral head, involving infraspinatus tendon. Strip-like high signal at subscapularis tendon insertion. Slightly high signal in proximal pulley part of long head of biceps tendon. Teres minor tendon morphology/signal unremarkable. Small amount of effusion in joint cavity and subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.

Medical History:?Over 10 years ago, a fall caused left shoulder dislocation (manual reduction + fixation). 8 years ago, pushing off a sofa caused recurrence. Over 20 recurrences followed (mostly self-reducible). Last episode 2+ months ago. No obvious daily pain, but cannot swim/play ball. Dislocation has occurred during sleep.

Anterior glenoid bone defect/flattening with surrounding avulsion fracture fragments. Type II acromion. Multiple patch-like high signals in humeral head. Increased signal in supraspinatus tendon near insertion. Infraspinatus, subscapularis, teres minor, and long head of biceps tendons unremarkable. Joint effusion.
